entry.title: Disable Credit Check in Custom Sales Process
entry.content:
Background

As shared in a previous blog, it is possible to create custom sales order types in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. This new innovation provides greater flexibility to fulfill customer business requirements in the Order‑to‑Cash process.
In this blog, I will share a real business use case and explain how to utilize custom sales order types to address customer requirements.

Customer Requirement

Supply company XYZ sells various products to customers. For certain customers, the credit check is required when selling high‑value products to ensure that sufficient credit limits are available before processing the sales order. However, for low‑value products, a credit check is not required in order to reduce the additional operational effort involved in order processing. The company XYZ also wants to have reporting to analyze sales data of these sales processes. 

Since only the standard sales order type “OR” is available to support the sell‑from‑stock process, a single sales order type is not sufficient to differentiate between sales processes for high‑value products and low‑value products. In addition, the credit check configuration is maintained at the sales document type level. Therefore, having different sales order types is a logical approach to support these distinct business use cases.

Solution 

Let's see how custom sales order type fulfills this requirement. 

Assume you are on SAP CBC and already finished the activity "Define Scope" and "Set Up Organization Structure". Now you can open the activity "Configure Standard Order Type" to define a new custom sales order type. 

Create a custom sales order type by copying standard type template XTA. 

Save and confirm the custom sales order type. Then deploy new custom sales order types to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Wait until the deployment is complete. 

Once custom sales order types are deployed to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, you can open the activity "Configure Your Business Processes", and search for SSCUI 102137 "Set Credit Limit Checks for Sales Document Types".

System will navigate to the SSCUI in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to configure the credit limit checks for sales document type. Find the entry of custom sales order type ZCRD and set the setting "Check Credit" to empty, which means no credit check. 

Save the configuration in SSCUI, and now the credit check is disabled for custom sales order type ZCRD. 

Log on with user "Internal Sales Representative" and open the app "Create Sales Order" to create a sales order with custom sales order type ZCRD. 

The sold-to party 10100005 is the customer who has credit limit maintained in the master data. 

Add the sales order item and save the sales order. 

Once sales order is saved, go to tab "Status and Blocks" and you will see this sales order is not blocked by credit check.

You can continue creating outbound delivery from the sales orders with custom sales order type ZCRD because no credit check is performed for such sales orders. 

Let's compare with the standard sales order type "OR". Create a sales order with sales order type "OR".

Use same sold-to party "10100005".

Add the item and save the sales order. A popup message is shown that credit limit is exceeded.

Go to tab "Status and Blocks", you will see the sales order with standard order type OR is blocked by credit check.

You need to work with Credit Controller to release the DCD (Documented Credit Decision) to get rid of the credit block on sales order to proceed the subsequent logistics processes. 

Conclusion

The introduction of custom sales order types is a significant innovation that greatly enhances flexibility in the Order‑to‑Cash process. It allows customers to define their own sales processes with minimal configuration effort to support different sales scenarios and process patterns.

With a single click, all relevant configurations from the standard sales order type “OR” are copied to the new custom sales order type, enabling customers to immediately execute the sell‑from‑stock process without any additional fine‑tuning. This capability empowers customers to adapt their sales processes more efficiently while reducing implementation complexity.

Please stay tuned for more template copy use cases, and feel free to submit your requirements through the Customer Influence Portal to help us prioritize our product roadmap.

entry.title: Re: G/L Account Short Description showing On Inputs and O...
entry.content: You will need to use Custom Fields and Logic or CDS views to bring the long description into the journal entry and line item apps. The standard system only shows short descriptions, so extension is the way to meet your requirement.

entry.title: Re: Homepage redesign customization level — SAP Signavio...

Hi @Andy_Feng9 , this community forum is an external forum for customers. Your question sounds rather internal so please use internal SAP compliant channels to get feedback from internals. 

Thank you 🙂
Dana

entry.title: Re: Building Low Code Extensions with Key User Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA and SAP Build
entry.content:
Great article, really liked how you explained the combination of key user extensibility with SAP Build in a practical way.

The point about empowering business users to create low-code extensions without heavy dependency on IT is especially relevant. It clearly reduces communication gaps and speeds up innovation cycles, which is something many organizations struggle with.

I also found the distinction between in-app (local) extensions and side-by-side extensions on SAP BTP very useful. Knowing when to use each approach is critical for maintaining a clean and scalable architecture.

Overall, a very insightful write-up on how organizations can leverage low-code capabilities in SAP S/4HANA to balance flexibility and governance. Looking forward to more such practical examples.

entry.title: Re: Optional Task: Use Joule CDS Unit Test Generation
entry.content: Package : ZAC_148863

entry.title: Re: How to access Environment Variables in Script Task/BP...
entry.content:
Hi Abhishek,

Environment Variables unfortunately cannot be accessed directly inside a Script Task in SAP Build Process Automation.
They are only resolved during deployment, so Script Tasks do not have a way to read them at runtime.

Recommended Approach:

Instead of using Environment Variables directly, you should:

  1. Create a Process Input Parameter
  2. Map your Environment Variable to Process Input Parameter during workflow deployment
  3. Access the parameter inside the Script Task via $.context.processInputs
    Hope this helps you — thanks!

entry.title: Re: Guide: Step By Step Procedure To Send And Receive Messages in a SAP Event Mesh Instance
entry.content:
Hi @sanj22,

you need to check the service descriptor of your messaging client. Did you allow to publish/consume the queue that you are trying to access? Maybe it is in a different namespace or you didn't allow it at all.

Regards,
Tobias

entry.title: questions about x-csrf-token for SAP OData APIs
entry.content:
I am integrating SAP APIs on the Feishu Integration Platform, and my SAP system version is S/4HANA Cloud. I have encountered several issues regarding the x-csrf-token for SAP OData APIs:
  1. According to the SAP website, the validity period of the token is 30 minutes. However, in Postman, the same token can be used for an entire afternoon without renewal, and all POST requests are successful.
  2. When sending multiple GET requests to fetch the x-csrf-token in Postman, the token remains unchanged (the same string). But when sending multiple GET requests via the Feishu Integration Platform, the token is renewed every time.
  3. After obtaining the x-csrf-token via GET on the Feishu Integration Platform, subsequent POST requests throw errors.
 
My questions are:
  1. What is the renewal mechanism of the x-csrf-token returned by SAP?
  2. Can I view inbound communication logs on the SAP website to facilitate debugging?
  3. For SAP OData API POST requests, is there a solution that does not require the x-csrf-token? The Feishu Integration Platform cannot implement the x-csrf-token logic.

entry.title: Re: Variance Option Missing in Optimized Story Bar Chart
entry.content:
@N1kh1l 

No, it wasn’t showing. But I finally got it working.

To add a variance, the chart needs to include a Time Calculation—only then does the variance option become available as an add‑on.

Greetings

entry.title: SAP Cloud Identity Services - Overview, Strategy and Roadmap
entry.content:
Identity is the backbone of every digital enterprise. In this session, discover how SAP Cloud Identity Services enable secure and seamless identity and access management across SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and connected SAP solutions.
We’ll explore SAP’s IAM strategy and how it brings together identity lifecycle management, authentication, single sign-on, and authorization management into a unified approach. You’ll also get a look at the roadmap, including upcoming innovations such as SAP-managed integration for SAP S/4HANA Cloud editions and support for emerging Agentic AI scenarios.

Join us to learn how SAP Cloud Identity Services help organizations strengthen security, simplify identity management, and support the next generation of intelligent applications. 🔐

Register

Live on YouTube

entry.title: Re: #10 - Review Cutover Plan - And The Importance for a Successful Go-Live
entry.content:
Great article , this really highlights how critical the cutover phase is for a successful go-live.

I particularly liked how you broke down the structure of pre-cutover, execution, and post-cutover activities. In real projects, many teams focus heavily on build and testing, but underestimate how important a well-defined cutover plan is.

From what I’ve seen, the biggest value of a strong cutover plan is in minimizing business disruption and ensuring data integrity during transition. Without clear timelines, ownership, and contingency planning, even a well-tested system can face issues at go-live.

Also, the emphasis on roles and responsibilities is very relevant cutover is a highly coordinated effort, and clarity here makes a big difference in execution.

Overall, a very practical and insightful write-up for anyone involved in SAP implementations. Looking forward to more such content!

entry.title: Re: element with types abap.dats and abap.dec optional in...
entry.content: The odata Version is V4

entry.title: NO Foreign trade reporting in the public cloud (like Z4, Z5, Z10)
entry.content:
In the Public Cloud, foreign trade reporting is no longer supported, and we now have to go through the cumbersome process of reactivating the old LZB indicator and defining it for each process in order to continue evaluating the required reports. We do not have FI-CA active. So far, we have not found any way to generate these reports automatically.

Since the LZB field is not mandatory, we will now change this and enforce it through a validation rule so that it becomes mandatory whenever a process needs to be reported. However, this workaround is not ideal, and from the Public Cloud I was really expecting more automation in this area.

Are we missing something?

entry.title: SAP Business AI in Public Sector - Partner live expert session
entry.content:
Public sector organizations are under pressure to do more with fewer resources, higher expectations, and constant change.  The opportunity?  AI that actually delivers impact.

In our upcoming SAP Business AI in Public Sector – Partner live expert session, we’ll walk through how you can help customers become more efficient, more responsive, and honestly, more future-ready.

We’ll start with a clear snapshot of the public sector landscape, what’s shifting, what matters.  Then we’ll go deeper into real AI use cases, plus how to position and sell these solutions effectively (because knowing what to sell is only half the story).

There’s also a live demo on SDE 2.0, always a favorite and time for your questions.

If you’re working with public sector clients, this session can sharpen your approach and open new conversations.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013345.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #PublicSector #BusinessAI #DigitalTransformation #GovTech

entry.title: Alternative Material Consumption in SAP ME & consumption posting in S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Hello All,

I have a scenario for Alternate component consumption done in SAP ME POD and consumption posting should be happened in S/4 HANA system

Scenario:
I have created a Production Order in S4 where i have maintained Two components and enabled Alt. Material for each other with usage probability to 50% for each of them(for e.g., Production Order 10001 with Qty 10, where component A is required to consume 10, since  i have maintained Alt components enabled for Component A & B, I have maintained Component A Qty to 10 with usage probability to 50% and Component B Qty to 10 with usage probability to 50% ). The order has been released to SAP ME. As expected i could see both the components in POD Component Assembly screen - for each SFC, Component A having Qty to assemble as 1 and Component B having Qty to assemble as 1. Simultaneously I have maintained Component B as the Alternative Material to Component A in Material Maintenance - Alternatives Tab.

Now I request your help to achieve any of the below case:
Case #1: How can i post component B consumption to SAP and is it possible to ignore the Component A without consuming in SAP ME POD(without disabling CT520)?

Case #2: Is it possible to send the consumption of Component B request to SAP without adding the Component B as Alternative in BOM in SAP at Production Order Level but if the Alternative material is maintained in Material Maintenance in SAP ME.

If there's a standard solution, Please let me where can i do the changes in the SAP/MES. If there it can be achieved via custom solution, appreciate the approach.

Hoping someone might have already done this 😋 🤞

Thank You!

entry.title: Re: he supplier down payment app is currently not visible...
entry.content:
Good day @paresh31837 

Thank you for your question.

For supplier down payment without a PO reference, you do not need SD or MM. Supplier downpayments processing happens in Accounts Payable Accounting. So, there you can manually manage your down payment requests and post outgoing payments.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/a9c0de2ae8c04ada95df67ccacc08152.html?locale=en-US

3629494 - 'Account 65001000 does not exist in chart of accounts YCOA' error in Manage Automatic Payments App - S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

The answer your question about the availability of an app to post supplier down payment without a purchase order, is Yes.

You should use:

  • Manage Supplier Down Payments Request app to create the supplier down payment request.

  • Post Outgoing Payments app to post the actual down payment against that request.

So, if you only see Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments, that is not the app for your scenario. That app is for PO based down payments. Since you require a non-PO supplier down payment, the relevant app are the ones mentioned above.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Integrate SAP Build Process Automation with Custom Business Object from S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Great blog—this is a very practical walkthrough of integrating SAP Build Process Automation with custom business objects in S/4HANA Cloud.

I especially liked how you combined in-app extensibility (Custom Business Object) with side-by-side workflow automation on SAP BTP. This hybrid approach is really powerful, as it keeps core logic inside S/4 while leveraging SAP Build for flexible process orchestration.

The step-by-step explanation of setting up communication arrangements and API triggers was also very helpful. In real scenarios, establishing reliable connectivity between S/4HANA and BTP is often the most critical (and sometimes overlooked) part.

The Bonus Plan approval use case is a great example it clearly shows how business users can trigger workflows and handle approvals seamlessly, while still maintaining control through backend logic.

Overall, this is a valuable guide for anyone looking to implement low-code workflow automation with clean extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Looking forward to more such detailed and practical scenarios!

entry.title: Secure, Compliant, and AI-Ready: The Future of Governance and Compliance on SAP BTP
entry.content:
Modern enterprises face increasing regulatory mandates around data protection, AI governance, customer interactions, and auditability. This session showcases how SAP BTP delivers a trusted foundation through an integrated set of compliance services—from anonymization and retention management to audit logging and AI-driven regulatory insights. Using a multi-industry demo scenario, participants will learn how to embed automated compliance into digital processes, reduce operational risk, accelerate audits, and safely unlock data for innovation and AI. Ideal for architects, security teams, product managers, and compliance stakeholders.

 

Register

Live on YouTube

Aurora

entry.title: Re: SAP FI – Document Currency Not Zero but Local Currenc...
entry.content: Yes, SAP requires consistency between document and local currency. The recommended fix is to reverse and repost correctly. There is no standard way to adjust document currency in place. The root cause is manual “local currency only” postings, so the preventive measure is to enforce proper upload templates or always use valuation transactions.

entry.title: Re: BOP is not working for MTO scenerio
entry.content: Hi Vun, the confirmation strategy used by Arvind is 'Improve', see also screenshot. However, also 'Redistributor' should do the work according to its definition. Regards, Andreas

entry.title: Re: Amount in Local currency is 0 in BAPI_GOODSMVT_ITEMS
entry.content:
Hi Mohammed,

Please note that in BAPI_GOODSMVT_ITEMS, the Amount in Local Currency field is expected to be populated automatically when you provide inputs such as Material Document and Year, as the system derives the value based on the document data.

Kindly share the screenshot for further review so we can analyze the behavior in more detail.

Best regards,

Aurora

entry.title: Unified, virtually transparent, enterprise ready technical connectivity on SAP BTP
entry.content:
Hybrid landscapes demand connectivity that is secure, modular, and easy to operate. Deep dive into central technical connection management with Destinations, explore patterns and best practices for automating complex authentication flows and X.509 client certificate renewal for mTLS, and learn how to easily expose any remote system, public or hosted on-premise/VPC, in a virtually transparent yet secure way in your local service mesh.

Register

Live on YouTube

entry.title: Re: Private Cloud automatic removal of dunning Level do n...
entry.content:
You are right to expect that once all open items are cleared, the dunning level should reset automatically. In SAP FI, however, the dunning level is not reduced simply by payment. It is only updated when a new dunning run is executed and the system recognizes that no items are overdue. That is why you saw no change even after running a cycle with no items to dunning. The SAP-standard solution is to schedule regular dunning runs (batch jobs) and ensure customizing allows level reset.

Also, what not to do is avoid manual resets in BP master data—this breaks audit compliance. Don’t rely on payments alone; SAP’s design is that dunning levels are controlled by the procedure, not by clearing.

entry.title: SAP BTP CloudFoundry and Kyma: Runtimes for a complete deveveloper experience
entry.content:
SAP BTP is currently offering two different application runtimes: CloudFoundry and Kyma. While they are two different technological stacks, they are two complementary runtimes targeting an harmonised user experience and consumption.

 

Register

Live on YouTube

entry.title: How to handle old open PM notifications after catalog profile and scoring changes?
entry.content:
• We use IW22 Task tab catalogs with parent parameter + child code
  • Earlier 17 parent parameters, now total 21 selections including 4 CI
  • Validation now enforces exactly 21 selections and single selection per parent
  • Old open notifications still show old parent parameters
  • New CI values and changed scores are not visible
  • Around 1300 notifications are open in OSNO/NOPR
  • New parent parameters were created instead of changing old ones
  • Need guidance on best approach for old open notifications

Questions

  1. Can existing notifications adopt new catalog structure/profile?
  2. If not, what is the best workaround?
  3. How should old-to-new score mapping be done?
  4. A major concern is that a child parameter needs to be deleted if that possible, kindly suggest.

entry.title: Re: What configuration is required for the intercompany p...
entry.content:
Document Flow (SD → FI)

  • Sales Order (created in selling company code).
  • Delivery (goods shipped from supplying company code).
  • Intercompany Billing (IV invoice):
    • Supplying company bills selling company.
    • FI entry: AR in supplying company / AP in selling company.
  • Customer Billing (F2 invoice):
    • Selling company bills end customer.
    • FI entry: AR in selling company / Revenue in selling company.

Accounting Entries

1. Customer Invoice (Selling Company → End Customer)
Selling Company Code

  • Dr Customer (Accounts Receivable)
  • Cr Revenue
  • Cr Output Tax (if applicable)

2. Intercompany Invoice (Supplying Company → Selling Company)
Supplying Company Code

  • Dr Intercompany Customer (Selling Company)
  • Cr Intercompany Revenue

Selling Company Code

  • Dr Intercompany Expense / COGS
  • Cr Intercompany Vendor (Supplying Company)

Payment Process

  • End customer pays selling company → clears AR in selling company.
  • Selling company pays supplying company → clears AP in selling company and AR in supplying company.
  • Net effect: revenue recognized in selling company, cost recognized from supplying company, and both company codes are balanced.

entry.title: Re: Warning Message in Financial Statement Versions App
entry.content:
Hello @test1234123 

The information on the exact cut-off date is not exactly publicized. 

Because of that, the recommended approach is for you to start the transition now, migrate your existing FSVs to Global Hierarchies, activated them there, and validate the related reporting before the old app is withdrawn.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: What Is Agentic AI and Why SAP Teams Are Paying Attention in 2026
entry.content:
You raise an important point about governance frameworks, and I agree that standardized baselines will be essential for adoption. But I think there's still a deeper architectural question that governance templates alone don't resolve.

Guardrails and validation checkpoints operate on the outputs of the agent's reasoning — they constrain what it can do, or they catch decisions that violate defined criteria after the decision has been generated. What they don't address is the reasoning process itself.

If the underlying architecture is LLM-based (which is the dominant pattern for agentic AI right now), the decision-making process is fundamentally opaque — not just to auditors reviewing after the fact, but to the system itself. The model doesn't have a mechanistically faithful account of why it chose Action A over Action B. It has a statistically generated output. Chain-of-thought traces are generated text, not introspective reports.

This means guardrails are essentially an acknowledgment that the system would make unacceptable decisions without them. They're a containment strategy, not a competence strategy. And they work for the failure modes you anticipated — but not for the novel situations where compliance boundaries matter most.

For mission-critical SAP environments, I think the harder question is: should we be implementing architectural approaches where the reasoning is inherently transparent and auditable, rather than wrapping opaque reasoning in governance layers? Because if we can constrain the action space tightly enough for the guardrails and wrappers to be reliable, we do not need an LLM in the reasoning loop at all — and if we can't so specify, the guardrails are still not sufficient. They still leave a reasoning chain the auditors cannot audit, and the machine cannot reliably reproduce.

To summarise the point, we have two options:
1. IF we can specify all the decision points accurately enough to reliably prevent agentic failures THEN we can implement a far cheaper and more reliable and auditable process automation solution.
2. IF we cannot so specify all the decision points to this degree of accuracy, THEN introducing an opaque, un-auditable, unpredictable probabilistic engine into the solution, AND THEN adding wrappers, guardrails, and etc to detect its failures and prevent them propagating (admittedly all good things) after the fact, does not seem to be an improvement. We are then spending more for less.

I think we owe it to ourselves and our customers to articulate clearly where the LLM-based reasoning layer adds value that cannot be achieved with fully transparent, deterministic architectures — and to be honest about the implementation and contingent costs when it doesn't.

The business case for LLM-based agents rests on the existence of a sweet spot: decisions too complex for rules engines but safe enough for opaque probabilistic reasoning. For mission-critical SAP workflows, I'd want to see that sweet spot precisely defined before we deploy them in our customer's businesses.

And if I cannot see that in our solutions, can anyone explain how our customers are going to?

entry.title: Re: Timesheet Time Entry with Multiple Activity Allocatio...

entry.title: Re: REFX-CLM-Public Cloud-Valuation contract -Transfer po...
entry.content:
The “Object” option (where you can assign a cost center or other CO object directly) is available in on‑premise, but in Public Cloud only “Divide Equally” is delivered as standard. When you assign a valuation rule, the system switches to the valuation tab and hides the distribution tab — this is by design in the cloud edition.

Instead of relying on the distribution tab, you assign the cost center directly in the condition line of the valuation contract. The periodic posting run will then automatically post to that cost center.

Anil

entry.title: Re: LE_SHP_MODIFY_ITEM Custom logic not working for chang...
entry.content: Hello @edoymaner, We marked the answer as accepted since there were no further updates on the topic after 7 days. If the answer provided was not helpful enough or you have further questions, you can unaccept it at any time. Best regards, Irene

entry.title: Re: Cross-Plant Actual Cost Roll-up
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA (including GROW/private cloud), CKMLCP does not perform a true cross‑plant actual cost roll‑up. Actual costing and Material Ledger valuation are always plant‑specific. Plant B will see the semifinished product from Plant A at the transfer price (or standard price), but the detailed cost breakdown from Plant A does not automatically roll up into Plant B’s finished product costing.

Having said that, accept that CKMLCP is plant‑specific. For consolidated cost visibility, design cross‑plant reporting rather than expecting CKMLCP to roll up costs. If the business requires Plant B to see Plant A’s actual breakdown, consider custom reporting or group costing approaches.

entry.title: Multiple Purchase Organization for one plant
entry.content:
We need to operate two separate purchasing organizations for a single plant to achieve a clear organizational separation of purchasing processes within the system. 
Is it technically possible in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to assign more than one purchasing organization to a single plant? If yes, what is the correct configuration in CBC? If no, what is the recommended approach to achieve the required organizational separation?

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP Trial Account RAP application's preview featu...
entry.content:
Hi @RachanaN_Ghanathe ,


I analyzed the issue and found that the 403 Forbidden error was occurring because the OData service was not properly configured in the communication setup.

To resolve this:

  • I created a Communication Scenario.
  • Added the Inbound Service (OData V4).
  • Assigned the service to the Communication Scenario.
  • Published it locally.

After completing these steps, the service is now working correctly, and the Fiori Elements app preview is loading without issues.

Please let me know if any further checks are needed.

entry.title: Re: Documet for E-Signature does not get created after su...
entry.content: Hi, I have added the mapping screenshot in P.S. for my question 🙂

entry.title: Re: Week 4 : Use Joule Predictive Code Completion to Implement Determination
entry.content: Week-4 completed for ZAC_2289322

entry.title: Re: Issue with Migration Transfer Date and Fiscal Year Cl...
entry.content:
Hi @Chris1973:
I wanted to follow up and let you know that I ran the job template via the Schedule Asset Accounting Jobs app as described in KBA 3730290, and it worked correctly. The fiscal year closing completed successfully and the system is now consistent with the intended migration setup.

Thank you once again for your thorough and clear explanation. Your guidance made it straightforward to resolve the issue.

Best regards,
Álvaro Fernández

entry.title: Need to develop batch program to transfer stock using movement type 311 relevant to WM
entry.content:
Hi ,

I need to develop a batch program to transfer stock from one storage location and storage bin to another storage location and storage bin in WM/EWM.

As part of my analysis, I first tried using I_MaterialDocumentTP, but I am unable to proceed because the storage bin field is not available there for posting.

I also explored the API /SCWM/IF_API_WHSE_TASK, but it appears to be restricted for use in the Public Cloud environment.

From my understanding, this stock transfer requirement might potentially be handled in one of the following ways:

  • By posting a stock transfer using movement type 311 along with Plant, Storage Location, Storage Bin, Batch, and Product details.

  • By changing the storage type using the Storage Attributes app.

Could you please suggest the appropriate solution for achieving this stock transfer in Public Cloud?

Thanks & Regards

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Need to develop batch program to transfer stock using...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

Could you please specify more detail about the requirement?

The 311 stock transfer is within the SAME warehouse number or different Warehouse number?

  • If it is about different warehouse number, Outbound delivery is required. It is not possible to finish it with sync goods movement.
  • If it is within the same warehouse number, you may set customizing "Activate Synchronous Goods Movements in the Warehouse" for Transfer Movement to prevent delivery creation, ODATA API API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV can be used for stock transfer.

 

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: oInventoryGenEntry creation error
entry.content: inventoryGenEntry.Reference2 = "employeeId" was the missing line

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud – Standard Model: Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields
entry.content:
With continuous efforts to enhance the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM) modeling experience, one of the most impactful enhancements that is newly introduced is the Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields capability. This feature enables a controlled and consistent way of mapping source fields with the environment fields which is used across functions and rules. For QA engineers, architects, and modelers alike, this means fewer discrepancies during execution, and cleaner model governance. In this article, I’ll walk through what this feature enhancement does, why it matters, and how it improves the overall modeling workflow in PaPM Cloud Standard Model.

What this feature enhancement does?

One key benefit of this enhancement is to provide an early warning to modelers in case of manually adding fields based on the source fields. If Source Fields data length is 50 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 50 or higher. Else, a validation will be seen, and modelers will not be able to finalize the mapping unless validation is fixed.

The rule, if Source Fields is with data length 23 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 23 or higher stays true. Therefore, in the fields dropdown what modelers will only see are the compatible environments fields.

This is also true for Key Figure fields for example DEC data type.

If Source Fields in this case the data length is 23 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 23 or higher. Else, a validation will be seen, and modelers will not be able to finalize the mapping unless validation is fixed.

In the event that modelers will be using fields dropdown option, meaning if there are existing environment fields in the modeling screen and modelers would like to reuse them to map the source fields, take note of the sources and environment fields Data Type, Data Length, and Data Decimals compatibility. In this example, for an existing DEC fields:

1 with lesser data length and decimals and 1 with higher data length and decimals.

The rule, if Source Fields is with data length 23 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 23 or higher stays true. Therefore, in the fields dropdown what modelers will only see are the compatible environments fields.

To know more about SAP PaPM Cloud SMs’ Data Types, maximum Data Length, and Data Decimals you may visit the Application Help Data Types, Master and Hierarchy Data.

Why it matters?

With the mapping enhancement in place, the goal is to promote consistent and compatible mapping between source and environment fields to avoid discrepancies during execution and to avoid mapping mistake such as choosing incompatible Data Type and mistakenly overwriting the existing Data Length or Data Decimals of a field which may results to inconsistent calculation.

How it improves the overall modeling workflow?

The keyword is consistency, when modelers have consistent mapping compatibility between source and environment fields, modelers will have the confidence that the data projected from any modeling functions are correct, formulas and selections are aligned, and execution of calculation to run smoothly eliminating the risk of having incorrect Data Type, Data Length, and Data Decimals.

I hope this article provides you with things you need to know about the enhanced Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields capability of SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model.

See you in the next!

entry.title: Re: How to find all tables of business user / employee
entry.content:
Hello @Kristina06 

Could you please also have a look on the following note?

SAP Note 3668500 - Reviewing application usage in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best regards,
Irene

entry.title: Re: Amount in Local currency is 0 in BAPI_GOODSMVT_ITEMS
entry.content:
Hello Mohammed,

The BAPI BAPI_GOODSMVT_GETITEMS does not list the Amount in Local Currency updated in MSEG-DMBTR.

See the corresponding documentation in the transaction BAPI.

Here navigate to:

Materials Management -> Inventory Management -> GoodsMovement -> GetItems -> GoodsmvtItems

Double clik on "GoodsmvtItems" and open the Documentation tab.

Here you have the available fields for this BAPI.

As you can see the field AMOUNT_LC is for "Externally entered posting amount in local currency", so this corresponds to the value in MSEG-EXBWR.

The BAPI BAPI_GOODSMVT_GETDETAIL also offers this same data.

In hope this clarifies!
Esther.

entry.title: Re: Extending Standard Timesheet v4 App Using SAPUI5 Adap...
entry.content: Ok thanks for your response but what about my requirement as i mentioned in this post.

entry.title: Re: Optional Task: Use Joule CDS Unit Test Generation
entry.content: Package: ZAC_880096

entry.title: Re: ABAP Development Tools for VS Code: Everything You Need to Know
entry.content:
Over the past years, the SAP development ecosystem has progressively expanded its tooling landscape—from the classical SAP GUI to Eclipse-based tools, and now towards integration with modern editors like Visual Studio Code.

From one perspective, this evolution reflects SAP’s effort to align with industry standards and meet developers where they already are. VS Code, with its lightweight architecture, extensibility, and widespread adoption, undoubtedly offers a compelling developer experience.

However, this raises an important question for the SAP community:

Are we moving toward greater flexibility, or increasing fragmentation?

Today, SAP developers find themselves navigating multiple environments:
- SAP GUI for traditional tasks
- Eclipse (ABAP Development Tools) for modern ABAP development
- VS Code for emerging integrations and cloud-centric workflows

While each tool brings advantages, the lack of a unified development environment can introduce complexity, context switching, and inconsistencies in the developer experience.

If we look at companies like Apple, they have built a cohesive and unified development ecosystem (e.g., Xcode), continuously evolving it while maintaining a strong identity and tight integration with their platform.

This leads to a broader reflection:

Should SAP continue embracing external tools and adapting them to its ecosystem?
Or should it invest in consolidating a unified, SAP-native development environment that evolves with modern standards while preserving consistency?

Of course, openness and integration are valuable. But so is coherence—especially in an ecosystem as complex and enterprise-critical as SAP.

Perhaps the real opportunity lies in finding the right balance:
- Leveraging modern, widely adopted tools
- While ensuring a seamless and consistent developer experience across the SAP landscape

I’m curious to hear how other developers perceive this shift.
Do you see VS Code integration as a step forward, or as another layer of fragmentation?

entry.title: Re: Why and When to use SAP Business Client
entry.content:
Hi Community,

           "

 

  • Use SAP BC on S/4HANA on-premise to use SAP Fiori apps and classic applications. This includes SAP GUI for Windows transactions with a harmonized visual experience via the SAP Fiori 2.0 Belize theme.
  • Use SAP BC if you do not want to lose your classic applications (Web Dynpro ABAP, Dynpro).

    "

     

    Thanks for your selfless sharing again, it really help us a lot.

    🙂

     

    Regards,

    ZFiori.

entry.title: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration - Alert Rules via SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Objective

The goal of this blog is to explain how to migrate your alert rules from SAP Process Orchestration and set up proactive monitoring for SAP Integration Suite exclusively using SAP Cloud ALM. We will cover the end-to-end setup including:

  • SAP BTP Cockpit configuration and endpoint creation.
  • Service and Endpoint configuration within SAP Cloud ALM.
  • Setting up alerts, filters, and event actions in Integration & Exception Monitoring.

Understanding the Transition to SAP Cloud ALM

When migrating your integration scenarios from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite, recreating your alerting mechanisms is a critical step. By using SAP Cloud ALM for your monitoring, you establish a connection point that improves visibility for all connected systems and ensures that integration errors are automatically detected and communicated.

Step 1: SAP BTP Cockpit Configuration

To connect SAP Cloud ALM with SAP Integration Suite, you must first create an endpoint in the SAP BTP Cockpit capable of retrieving Message Logs and Artifact Health data.

  • Navigate to your Subaccount from the Global Account in SAP BTP Cockpit.
  • Go to Services -> Instances and Subscriptions and choose Create.
  • Select the Process Integration Runtime Service and choose the integration-flow plan.

 

  • Add the following roles to the instance: MonitoringDataRead and MonitoringArtifactsDeploy.

 

  • After creating the instance, open it to create a Service Key.
  • Provide a descriptive name, set the Key Type to Client ID/Secret, and save the key in JSON format securely, as it will be required in later steps.

Step 2: SAP Cloud ALM Configuration

With the BTP endpoint prepared, the next phase is establishing the connection in SAP Cloud ALM. The user performing this needs specific roles: x_landscape_Administrator and x_landscape_SecurityAdministrator.

  • Access Administration and navigate to Landscape Management.

 

  • Under Services & Systems, you can attempt to automatically import your service by navigating to Configuration -> Import of Subscribed SAP Services.

 

 

  • If an automatic import isn't possible (which typically happens if the daily synchronization job hasn't run yet, the service is registered under a different customer number, or local access controls restrict your visibility), you can manually add a New Service. To do this, define parameters such as the Name and the System Number. To find your System Number, log into SAP for Me, navigate to 'Portfolio & Products' > 'My Product Portfolio', click on your specific product, and look under the 'Systems' tab for the tenant's unique identifier. Finally, select the Service Type (SAP Integration Suite) and input the Root URL / Logon URL that you copied from your saved Service Key.

 

  • Once the service is created, select Add in the Endpoints section and paste the Service Key details you saved earlier to automatically fill the parameters, then click Check Connection.

 

 

Step 3: Setting Up Alerts and Notifications

Now that the external systems are connected, you can configure the actual alert rules using the Integration & Exception Monitoring section in Operations.

To perform this, users need roles such as ops_im_IntegrationArchitect, ops_im_IntegrationOwner, or x_calm_GlobalAdministrator.

  • Select your newly configured service from the Managed Components list (under Services) and click Apply.

 

 

  • Access Edit Configuration for the service and turn on Data Collection to begin gathering monitoring data.

     

 

 

 

  • Navigate to Events to add the specific events used for alerts.

 

 

  • For individual Integration Flow exceptions, add an event and set the Event Name to "Erroneous CPI Message Detected (Single)". To monitor specific messages or restrict events to certain integration flows, you can create custom filters. In the Filters tab, you can define filter parameters for specific integration flows. For example, you can limit the alert to a single interface by specifying its Artifact Name or Artifact ID, or include multiple interfaces by providing a list of names. Furthermore, you can use powerful combinations by pairing the Artifact Name with specific Statuses or Error Messages (e.g., only triggering the alert if 'iFlow_A' fails with an HTTP 500 error).

 

  • You can define specific parameters (like triggering the event for errors older than 5 minutes) and use the Filters tab to restrict the events to specific integration flows.

 

Post-Configuration Activities: Event Actions and Customizing Output

Just like manual fine-tuning steps in a interface migration, your event routing will need a few final touches:

 

  • Select verified recipients from the Recipient section. If your recipients aren't listed, add them by going to SAP Cloud ALM > Operations > Notification Management, clicking the '+' icon, and entering the target email addresses separated by commas.

 

  • When an error matches your defined parameters and filters, the system will automatically trigger an email notification containing key details like the integration artifact, message ID, status, and timestamp.

 

  • For single erroneous messages, you can highly customize the information displayed in the email up to 7 fields by choosing the appropriate attributes on the Grouping tab during Event configuration.

 

Conclusion

Migrating your alert rules to SAP Cloud ALM simplifies the monitoring process while guaranteeing that your SAP Integration Suite landscape remains highly visible. By establishing a secure BTP endpoint and defining granular event filters and notification actions, you can ensure reliable, scalable, and proactive error handling for all your newly migrated integration interfaces.

For additional questions related to the SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite migration, see FAQ – Migration from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: Re: How to Enable Translations for RAP-based Fiori Apps i...
entry.content:
Hello,

RAP applications use several layers of texts:

  • CDS / RAP BO annotations (e.g., @EndUserText.label)
  • i18n front‑end texts (if using freestyle UI5 or adaptation projects)
  • Launchpad catalog, tiles, and spaces/pages
  • Developer extensibility objects (data elements, messages, DDIC elements, metadata extensions)

In S/4HANA Public Cloud, SAP provides dedicated tools for each of these translation areas.


Regards,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: 5 Things You Need to Know Before Starting with SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hey! It's wonderful to hear that 🙂 Yes. You can start learning journeys on our learning hub platform or join any group with the topic that interests you more SAP Learning Groups | SAP Community.

If you would like to join any of our online events to gain specific knowledge, check this website: Eventos de SAP | Buscador

entry.title: Re: Homepage redesign customization level — SAP Signavio...
entry.content: Hi, if you are unsure if the design is compliant with SAP guidelines it might be good to open a compliance ticket. Otherwise, your team or your manager might be a good source to get further directions. But unless you do not need feedback from customers, the SAP Signavio community is the wrong place imho.

entry.title: Early Validation Gap
entry.content:
We ran into a situation during an ECC to S/4HANA migration where validation was technically “planned,” but practically happening too late.

Most issues only showed up during mock loads:

  • Business teams flagged mismatches late

  • Rework cycles increased

  • Timelines started slipping

What helped:

We shifted validation earlier into the process—before mock loads—by running smaller, iterative checks aligned with business rules.

This reduced surprises significantly and made business sign-off smoother.

Curious —

Are you validating continuously during migration, or still relying on mock cycles?

entry.title: Everything You Always Wanted to Know About InnerSource Adoption in Your Organization*
entry.content:
How widely is InnerSource really adopted in your organization - and what’s holding teams back? In this post, we explore how a well-designed, representative survey can cut through assumptions, reveal the true state of InnerSource adoption, and uncover the obstacles an InnerSource Program Office (ISPO) needs to address.

Why an InnerSource Survey Matters

InnerSource transformations do not succeed simply by promoting best practices or inspiring a handful of motivated teams - they succeed when the organization as a whole understands and embraces the model. Yet one of the biggest challenges for any InnerSource Program Office (ISPO) is understanding the real state of InnerSource adoption across the company.

By the nature of their work, ISPO’s and Open Source Program Offices (OSPO’s) primarily interact with people who are already familiar with Open Source principles or who are naturally enthusiastic about InnerSource. This might create an unintended “expert bubble”: a circle of colleagues who already understand and appreciate the value of transparency, reuse, and cross-team collaboration. While these individuals are essential champions, they might represent only a fraction of the overall organization. As a result, the ISPO’s perception of InnerSource maturity can easily become skewed - often appearing much more advanced than what the broader company reality reflects.

A structured, organization-wide InnerSource survey helps break out of this bubble. It provides the ISPO with an unbiased, data-driven view of what is actually happening.

Moving Beyond Assumptions

Without structured data, assessments of InnerSource adoption often depend on anecdotal evidence or impressions. A survey enables a realistic evaluation of awareness, participation, and perceived challenges.

Managers value measurable, verifiable information. Survey data enables the ISPO to communicate clearly and convincingly with leadership. Instead of narratives or isolated success stories, the ISPO can present factual insights about adoption levels, challenges, and opportunities. This not only strengthens the credibility of the InnerSource initiative but also helps justify budget, staffing, and strategic support.

Understanding Awareness, Barriers, and Communication Channels

A well-designed InnerSource survey goes far beyond asking who uses InnerSource. It helps uncover why people do - or do not – adopt InnerSource practices. For instance:

  • Are employees aware of what InnerSource is?
  • Do they know how to get started?
  • Do they feel confident contributing to other teams’ codebases?
  • Which organizational, technical, or skills-related barriers stand in the way?

These insights are critical. They allow the ISPO to tailor trainings, documentation, and enablement efforts to what teams actually need, not what the ISPO thinks they might need.

Effective communication is a cornerstone of any InnerSource rollout. But communication only works if people see it, understand it, and find it useful. A survey helps evaluate the ISPO’s communication channels: Are internal newsletters reaching developers? Is the documentation platform known and accessible? Are community calls perceived as valuable? These answers ensure that ISPO outreach is not only active, but impactful.

Data That Enables Strategic Focus

The insights gained from an InnerSource survey can provide an essential foundation for ISPO strategy and planning. A representative survey delivers quantitative data across different business units, countries, and locations, giving the ISPO a clear view of where InnerSource is thriving and where it is still largely unknown or unused. This level of granularity allows the ISPO to sharpen its focus and allocate efforts where they will have the greatest impact.

This enables:

  • Activity planning: Identifying which topics require more training, mentoring, or enablement.
  • Targeted focus: Understanding differences between units and regions helps the ISPO prioritize specific countries, locations, or organizational domains.
  • Performance management: Survey results form a baseline from which the ISPO can define meaningful goals and KPIs for itself.

With this data-driven foundation, the ISPO can move from broad, generic initiatives to precise, strategic actions that align with organizational needs and maximize value.

Designing an Effective InnerSource Survey

A well‑structured survey requires clarity about the target groups and objectives.

Separate Surveys for Developers and Managers

InnerSource adoption relies heavily on two distinct groups - developers and middle managers - each with very different responsibilities, perspectives, and challenges. Because their roles differ fundamentally, they require separate surveys tailored to their needs. SAP, therefore, runs two dedicated surveys – one targeting colleagues with development roles, one targeting middle managers (i.e., management levels L2, L3, …) - and alternate them each year: developers one year, managers the next.

Developers work directly with code, collaboration processes, and repositories, so they experience InnerSource at the most practical level. Their survey focuses on awareness, contribution behavior, and barriers that prevent participation. This provides insight into the day‑to‑day reality of InnerSource adoption.

Middle managers shape the environment in which InnerSource can succeed by defining priorities, resources, and team expectations. Their survey assesses support for InnerSource, perceived benefits, and organizational or structural blockers. This helps the ISPO understand leadership alignment and readiness across units.

Survey Structure

Every survey begins with the question: "Do you know what InnerSource is?". Since we cannot assume that every survey participant knows what InnerSource is, but we want to enable everyone to answer the remaining questions, we provide a brief explanation of InnerSource along with a short example.

The survey includes three sections:

  1. General Perception and ISPO Communication: This part collects insights into how InnerSource is perceived overall and how well ISPO communication channels are known and used.
  2. InnerSource Contributions: This section examines whether individuals or teams contribute to InnerSource projects and why contributions happen or do not happen.
  3. Application of InnerSource in Own Projects: Here the survey investigates whether teams use InnerSource practices in their own repositories, such as openness to contributions or the presence of contribution guidelines.

Free‑text fields allow respondents to provide nuance, examples, and concerns that predefined choices would miss. Although they require more evaluation effort, they often surface the most valuable insights.

To enable comparison of survey results across years, we keep the questions as stable as possible. This allows us to identify trends.

Each survey is sent to a randomized, representative group of colleagues. Responses remain anonymous, but include structural attributes such as location, country, organizational unit, and manager type (Manager Manages Teams / Manager Manages Managers – MMT/MMM) to support drill‑down analysis.

What We Learned and How SAP’s ISPO Responded

Running regular InnerSource surveys provides valuable insights - but the real impact comes from what an organization does with those insights. At SAP, the ISPO has used survey results not only to assess the current state of InnerSource adoption, but to define clear, targeted actions that address the most important challenges identified by developers and managers. Below, we present some of the key findings from SAP’s surveys and the initiatives derived from them.

High Perceived Value - but Low Awareness

One of the clearest survey signals was that both developers and managers strongly associate InnerSource with important benefits, such as increased developer efficiency, improved cross-team collaboration, and higher reuse of code and solutions.

At the same time, a surprisingly large share of respondents indicated that they do not know what InnerSource is. Free‑text answers reinforced this point: many participants held misconceptions about InnerSource or stated that they were “not familiar with the open source way of working.”

To address this gap between perceived value and actual understanding, the SAP ISPO launched several initiatives:

  • More training and education focused on practical InnerSource skills
  • Inclusion of InnerSource in internal curricula for developers, architects, and related roles
  • Sharing concrete examples through SAP-internal InnerSource patterns, use cases, and success stories (see [3])
  • Presentations and awareness sessions across units, locations, and communities

These efforts aim to ensure that the benefits people associate with InnerSource can actually be realized in practice.

Awareness Varies Significantly Across Locations

One of the most striking findings was the large difference in InnerSource awareness across SAP’s major development locations. Some regions showed comparatively high familiarity with InnerSource, while others had very low recognition of the concept.

To address these regional disparities, the ISPO is working on strengthening local engagement structures:

  • Connecting directly with local Managing Directors at development locations
  • Starting regional InnerSource communities to build local momentum
  • Introducing regional InnerSource ambassador programs to provide on‑the‑ground support and advocacy

These regional initiatives help tailor awareness activities to local cultures, priorities, and development ecosystems.

Developers Struggle to Find InnerSource Projects

A frequently mentioned barrier was the difficulty of discovering relevant InnerSource projects. Developers who were motivated to contribute often did not know where to find suitable projects or how to identify opportunities aligned with their skills.

To solve this, SAP launched the InnerSource Project Portal - a centralized internal platform listing all InnerSource projects (see [1], [5]). The portal allows contributors to browse projects by topic, technology, or domain, search for contribution opportunities, identify active InnerSource initiatives across the organization. This reduced one of the most practical barriers to participating in InnerSource.

Insufficient Incentives Were Seen as a Major Blocker

Another recurring theme was a perceived lack of incentives for InnerSource participation. Some respondents felt their contributions were not recognized.

To improve recognition and increase the visibility of InnerSource contributions, SAP is planning to introduce an InnerSource award. The award will highlight and acknowledge outstanding InnerSource projects, significant cross-team contributions, and sustained efforts to strengthen collaboration and knowledge sharing. Its purpose is to create positive visibility, encourage participation, and reinforce that InnerSource work is valued across the organization.

Conclusion

Well designed InnerSource surveys offer the data and insights required to uncover barriers, steer targeted improvements, and strengthen the effectiveness of an organization’s InnerSource initiatives. By translating survey results into targeted actions, ISPO's are able to directly address challenges.

Further Information

[1] InnerSource & Discoverability (InnerSource Commons Community Call, July 2021)

[2] Establishing InnerSource at SAP (SAP blog post, October 2021)

[3] Cultivating InnerSource at SAP (SAP blog post, January 2024)

[4] InnerSource Contribution Insights (Presentation at InnerSource Commons Summit, November 2025)

[5] InnerSource Portal (InnerSource Commons Pattern)

*But Maybe Were Afraid to Ask

entry.title: Re: Week 3 : Use RAP Business Logic Prediction to Implement Validation
entry.content: Week 3 Challange is done - Package : ZGE295999

entry.title: Re: Timesheet Time Entry with Multiple Activity Allocatio...
entry.content:
Hello @S0025758586

Apologies for the delayed response.

Wha did I mean by “copying” in Q2?

I meant the standard Copy Time Entries function in timesheet which lets you copy existing time entries from selected source days or weeks to target days or weeks. Your understanding is correct that in standard, one timesheet record is not automatically transformed into multiple activity allocations such as T001, T002 and T003 from the same 13:00 to 15:00 entry.

So, for your scenario, the practical standard options remain:

  • For you to create multiple time entries if you need multiple activity type postings, or
  • You use a periodic allocation approach for indirect costs rather than trying to derive several activity types from one timesheet source record.

You see D10, D20, D30 and not D110 in Overhead Calculation because Public Cloud uses simplified and predefined standard content in many controlling configuration areas. In other words, D110 is not configurable in Public Cloud.

Because of that, your observation is consistent with the behaviour in Public Cloud, which provides costing sheet logic, but the design is more restricted than on premise/private cloud, and basically not giving you that same flexibility for fine grained per WBS dependency logic or template driven derivation that you are looking for.

So, if your requirement is to derive several indirect cost components from one labour-based source entry and post them separately to WBS with project specific flexibility, then Public Cloud does not that in the same way as production activity confirmation scenarios. The better alternative is Universal Allocation, where you can allocate plan or actual values from sender to receiver.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Questions About Display Options in the New SAP Succes...
entry.content:
@Joe0603 

The View Details might appear if you have table fields defined in your Goal Plan, such as Milestones or Metric Lookup Table. In the Legacy version these would show on the Other Details tab, whereas the Goal Details tab could be controlled via form-layout configuration. 

Now, the visibility of these fields is based on the field-permissions that the users have, although you can still use tab-permissions to control the visibility of the tabs.

Hope this will help, 

Regards, 

Jordi

entry.title: Re: Homepage redesign customization level — SAP Signavio...

entry.title: OpenText archiving and document access (ADA) Bandwidth
entry.content:
Hi,

Has anyone experienced bandwidth overuse with OpenText Archiving and Document Access (ADA) that required a top-up? If so, could you share your experience?

Thank you !

entry.title: Re: Group-Assignment via Entra ID in SAP Cloud Identity Services
entry.content: Is there any news on this?

entry.title: Does Fiori with same function :Accounts Document Output China in Japan?
entry.content:
I know there is an app for printing accounting vouchers. (Accounts Document Output China. Transaction:IDCNDOC_CLD) in SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Is there an app with this function available in Japan? If not, what are some ways to print them?

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Fund Management - MIR7 Parked documents Deletion
entry.content:
Follow the below steps

  • Identify all parked documents created before FM activation.
  • Decide whether they should be posted (with FM coding added) or deleted.
  • Use FBV2/FBV3 or Manage Parked Journal Entries app to process them.
  • For large volumes, raise a ticket with SAP to explore technical clean‑up options.

entry.title: Re: Multiple Purchase Organization for one plant
entry.content: You cannot assign two purchasing orgs to one plant in S/4HANA Public Cloud. The correct approach is to use purchasing groups, roles, and custom fields to separate processes within a single purchasing org. If absolute separation is required, you’d need to model two plants instead.

entry.title: Re: Week 4 : Use Joule Predictive Code Completion to Implement Determination
entry.content: Week 4 Challange is done . Package : ZGE295999

entry.title: Re: Year end Activities of MM
entry.content: Year‑end activities in SAP MM focus on closing open purchase orders, reconciling inventory, and ensuring valuation consistency. The key tasks include clearing GR/IR accounts, checking open POs and deliveries, reconciling stock values with FI, and carrying forward balances. These activities are coordinated with FI, CO, SD, PP, and PM to ensure a clean financial close.

entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Performance (Forms): Option to disable...
entry.content:
Hi @JDLidl 

The ability to show section descriptions expanded by default is coming on 1H 2026 Release, with a new option in Form Template Settings > Expand Form Section Descriptions on First Opening (only applicable to the latest form experience).

Additionally, the Show More is replaced by an arrow that can be selected to expand/collapse the descriptions as needed.  This is already published on the What's New Viewer (Early Access) for SAP Partners since March 27th. The What's New Viewer general access will be available in April 3. 

Hope this will help, 

Regards, 

Jordi

entry.title: Re: How to copy one sales order to another sales order in...
entry.content: Use VA01 → Create with Reference and ensure copy control is set to carry configuration. The BOM will be regenerated based on the copied configuration values.

entry.title: Re: Questions : March 2026 Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers in ABAP
entry.content: Thanks for completing the challenge. No need to submit. we have the Community ID which was used in the package for assigning the badges.

entry.title: Re: Questions : March 2026 Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers in ABAP
entry.content: You can continue but, no support will be provided for any issues.

entry.title: Re: Homepage redesign customization level — SAP Signavio...
entry.content: Hi Andy, please contact me internally via Teams

entry.title: Re: Optional Task: Use Joule CDS Unit Test Generation
entry.content: Hi @Mohit_Kumar991 : User will be valid for 30 days.

entry.title: Re: Questions : March 2026 Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers in ABAP
entry.content: /consume is not part of the challenge and no support will be provided. It does work for me but, many have reported issues.

entry.title: Re: VIM 7.5 ECC – MRKO Posting Does Not Finalize DP Workf...
entry.content:
SAP/OpenText do not support bypassing VIM’s posting framework. If you need MRKO, you must wrap it inside VIM’s posting classes so the DP document is updated and workflow closed. Otherwise, you will always end up with active workflows and broken analytics.
Having said that, the fix is not in MRKO itself, but in ensuring VIM’s DP finalization logic runs after MRKO. You need to call the VIM posting APIs to update BUS_OBJTYPE/KEY and close the workflow.
